title: ACLU and Protect Democracy Sue DHS Over Secret 'Home Entry Memo' Trained at FLETC
excerpt: The ACLU, Protect Democracy, and co-counsel filed a federal lawsuit challenging ICE's secret May 2025 directive authorizing warrantless home entries, alleging the policy was secretly incorporated into ICE recruit training at FLETC while instructors were told not to document that training.
content:
On April 2, 2026, Protect Democracy, the ACLU, ACLU of Minnesota, ACLU of D.C., and Dorsey & Whitney filed suit against DHS, ICE, and the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center on behalf of six immigrants and U.S. citizens who had been subjected to warrantless forced home entries.

The complaint challenges ICE's secret internal memo titled "Utilizing Form I-205, Warrant of Removal" — the "Home Entry Memo" — signed by Acting Director Todd Lyons on May 12, 2025. The memo authorizes ICE agents to forcibly enter and search homes using only an administrative form signed by a DHS official, not a judge. The complaint alleges DHS deliberately concealed the policy: agents were instructed to review the memo in a supervisor's presence, return it without making copies, and not document that they had been trained on it. The memo was exposed by whistleblowers in January 2026. Whistleblower Ryan Schwank confirmed at his February 23 congressional testimony that he had been ordered to train new cadets under the memo.

  • Lead plaintiff Teyana Gibson Brown, a U.S. citizen in Minneapolis, described armed agents using a battering ram to break down her door, pointing military-grade rifles at her family, including her nine-year-old daughter
  • No court has ever upheld a general authority for warrantless home entries in the immigration enforcement context, according to legal experts
  • The lawsuit argues the policy reverses more than two decades of ICE's own training materials and operational guidance that prohibited warrantless home entries
  • An estimated 1.6 million people are subject to the new policy, according to the complaint
  • Six former DHS General Counsels spanning multiple administrations co-signed a New York Times op-ed calling on the government to end warrantless home entries
mediaPlaceholder: Federal complaint and ICE 'Home Entry Memo' Form I-205 policy document filed as exhibit in the lawsuit
